focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

Blue, salted morning leaked through the blinds and across the defense folder on Leela's desk while the radiator hammered the pipe behind it. The noise had started at six and never settled back into mere noise. She had given up on sleep, shoved the blanket aside, and crossed the studio barefoot, the floorboards cold enough to bite. Her right hand had curled during the night. She pulled the wrist brace tighter with her left, listening to the Velcro rasp in the dark.

The folder held exactly what she had left there: dissertation abstract, chapter summaries, projected timeline, the one-page defense calendar she had built and rebuilt until every week between March and June looked occupied by progress instead of panic. She had printed the calendar on better paper than the rest because the department copier made ordinary sheets feel temporary. If today went the way it was supposed to go, she would email the graduate coordinator by four, hold a room for late April, and stop opening the graduate-housing portal just to stare at the line about summer vacancy. If today went well, the credit-card minimum on the eighteenth would remain what it had been for months, a nuisance rather than a referendum. If today went well, the money her mother had sent in November could stay filed under bridge instead of beginning to look like subsidy.

She boiled water for coffee she did not really want, then drank it because her body no longer recognized the difference between ritual and appetite. The mug warmed her palm through the chipped glaze. She ate half a banana gone soft at the stem and swallowed an antacid that turned to chalk at the back of her tongue. Her phone, faceup beside the sink, gave her nothing from June. The last exchange sat there from two nights earlier: `committee...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...chronology, the cover memo. The empty space where the September captures should have sat in the exhibit list looked obscene now that all three of them had named it. She addressed the email to Gloria Fisk and the graduate studies grievance address, then copied the ombuds office because June insisted on one witness outside the department and Omar said witnesses mattered more than hope.

The subject line looked flimsy no matter how she phrased it.

"Keep grievance," Omar said. "And keep misuse."

"Don't write concerns," June said. "We're already protecting him enough."

Leela typed: Graduate grievance regarding supervisory misconduct and misuse of student research.

She re-read the attachments, not because she might change her mind, but because not changing it had become the whole shape of the morning.

"Send it," June said.

Leela clicked.

The message held on-screen while the wall clock knocked off another second and Omar's thumbnail clicked once against the desk. Then it vanished into Sent. The auto-response arrived fifty-three seconds later, chilled and neutral, thanking them for their submission and promising review according to university procedure. Dawn had begun somewhere outside the basement windows too high to see from here. The fluorescent lights still made everybody look sick.

Leela shut the laptop. Server hum filled the room, along with the cheap wall clock clicking toward six.

She slid the envelope back into the inner sleeve of her backpack.

June watched her do it. Omar looked at the whiteboard instead. The complaint now existed in three inboxes and at least two offices, brave enough to start a fight and weak enough to lose one. Whatever they had been while they built it did not survive the omission intact, and nobody in the room believed send meant done.
